data.title: ASML Is Approaching My Buy Price - Here Is When I'll Start Buying
data.description:
Introduction

Perhaps the most important thing for investors to understand about themselves is the level of medium and long-term returns they are trying to achieve. If your returns goals are not ambitious, then far more stocks will look like good values at higher prices. In that situation, the investor mostly just needs to focus on the quality of the business rather than price and valuation because the primary goal is to prevent losses rather than achieve above-average returns. However, if an investor is seeking superior medium-term and long-term returns, the task of selecting stocks becomes more challenging (as it should). One needs to be very careful about what they are paying for the stocks they buy. I am one of those investors who aim for higher-than-average long-term returns. My baseline goal is an 8% CAGR plus inflation. (And because there will always be some losers in the mix, the threshold of expected returns for individual purchases usually needs to be even higher than that baseline to make up for the inevitable underperformers.) While during the current bull market, these levels of returns haven't been particularly hard for an index like the S&P 500 (SPY) to achieve, over the long term, it has been more difficult.

Since inception, even with the market at all-time highs, SPY has returned slightly under the 8% real return goal, with a 7.94% real annual return. The only periods since the dot.com crash SPY has exceeded this goal were the 2007 peak before the 2008 recession and the 2022 peak before inflation had worked its way into the economic data. Roughly 19 of the past 20 years, SPY hasn't achieved an 8% real CAGR when measured from the inception of the ETF in the mid-1990s.

Only the best investors, like Warren Buffett, have been able to navigate the dot.com bust and the GFC and do better than an 8% real CAGR during most time frames. Ray Dalio is another, but his Pure Alpha Fund return data are harder to come by. Most of the estimates I've seen show that Bridgewater's fund has had similar returns since the mid-1900s to those of Berkshire.

Why do I bring this up in an ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) article, you might ask? I do so because the most common critique of my strategies is that my buy prices are too low and will never occur. I partially understand this critique because if one only reads my public articles, it seems like I don't buy many stocks. But the reality is that since I started my Investing Group, The Cyclical Investor's Club, in 2019, I've purchased over 160 positions. It's just that only a small portion of those make it into articles outside the service, and since 2021, an even smaller portion than normal have done so. Another misconception seems to arise because, in a disproportionate number of my articles, I am warning investors about downside risks they might not have considered. This can make it seem like I am perpetually afraid, and hoarding cash in my mattress, waiting for the next big calamity before buying anything. But even though I've mostly been in a "risk off" mode since early 2022, in the past 12 months I've purchased 23 positions.

The truth is that I spend a lot of time studying individual stock histories because historical patterns can be a good guide to the future. Human psychology hasn't changed for thousands of years, and the economic system in the West hasn't changed much for about 500 years, except for central banking, which came along a bit over 300 years ago. When we put these facts together, historical patterns, particularly those that occurred outside of recent history, have a lot of predictive power. And history is full of situations, both for markets and individual companies, when it was precisely the point at which observers thought downside risk had been eliminated, or was out-of-mind, when these markets and companies reached their zenith and started to decline or crash. The conditions in the semiconductor industry have been ripe with this sort of thinking since AI-themed investing captured the imagination of investors a couple of years ago, ASML included.

History can help frame what is possible for businesses and stocks in the future. So in this article, I will share ASML's historical earnings and stock price patterns, and use them to help guide me to a strategy that has a high probability of producing the high medium-term returns I aim for as an investor, while also reducing the depth of potential drawdowns.

Historical Earnings Cyclicality

The first task with any stock is to analyze and categorize the historical earnings patterns. The key question I want to know is how cyclical earnings have been. This informs me which investing strategy is best suited for the stock.

I have circled the years in red in the FAST Graph above where ASML had negative earnings growth. We can see that they experienced a very deep earnings drawdown in 2008/9 that resulted in fully negative earnings. They also experienced negative earnings following the dot com crash, though it is not pictured on the graph. Most declines since the last real recession in 2009 have been shallow to moderate in nature.

When businesses have a pattern of earnings declines that exceed -50%, and then have those earnings recover in a timely manner, I classify those businesses as "Deep Cyclicals". This classification is important because when a business has deeply cyclical earnings, traditional earnings or free cash flow-based analyses are not very useful at predicting the medium-term movements of the stock price. For example, in 2007 ASML had a P/E ratio of around 23 near its peak. It had almost the same P/E ratio of 22 at the March 2009 bottom, even though the stock price had dropped about -65%. This is because earnings had also fallen about -60% at that point. A 22-23 P/E would have both been a horrible valuation to buy the stock...and a great valuation to buy the stock. In other words, earnings were not very useful to help determine a good price to pay for ASML due to its deep earnings cyclicality.

Because of this dynamic, once it has been established a business has deeply cyclical earnings, I use historical stock price patterns to help estimate good prices at which to buy the stocks as a medium-term investment.

Historical ASML Price Cyclicality

Because I cover and monitor hundreds of stocks, I perform my stock analyses in stages. First, I classify what type of business I'm dealing with, and whether there is enough historical data to be useful. If I establish it's a business I can reasonably analyze with some confidence, and it fits the parameters of one of my strategies, then, I establish a buy price where if everything checks out, I would be willing to take a position. If the stock price eventually gets close to my buy price, I do deeper research to see if there is anything that might prevent me from buying the stock. This is the opposite of how most other investors do things. Most investors (if they conduct any research at all) perform lots of research on the business before they consider buying the stock or examining the valuation. I find this is often wasted time because many of the best businesses can go years without trading at reasonable valuations that would entice me to buy them. And I need good valuations to achieve the above-average returns I'm looking for.

Above is a historical stock price drawdown chart for ASML that goes back to the mid-1990s. We see that the stock experienced a roughly -65% drawdown in 1998 before fully recovering by 2000, and then experiencing a massive -90% decline. Ultimately, it took about 12 years for ASML to recover its 2000 peak price. This is important information for investors to know because it shows what can happen after a super-cyclical peak (or bubble). Typically, I want a cyclical stock to recover its previous peak within about 5 years. Usually, I only buy deep cyclical stocks when they are at least -50% off their highs, so if the price recovers within 5 years, I get a 100% return on my investment, which is roughly a 15% CAGR. If it takes longer than 5 years to recover, as ASML did from 2000 to 2012, my annual returns would be much lower, so I tend to avoid longer-recovery stocks unless I can buy them very cheap.

I'll write more about super-cycles later in the article, but for now, I'm going to set that long post-2000 recovery off to the side and shorten our time frame to 2006 so we can get a more recent read on ASML stock behavior during downcycles.

ASML's more recent history shows about a -65% drawdown in 2008/9 with a 3-year recovery time. This, of course, occurred during a very bad recession. Then, we also have a -55% decline in 2021/2 that did not include a recession. The recovery time from that decline was about 2 years. Currently, the stock price is down about -37% off its recent highs from this summer.

I think, all other things roughly equal, we can use these previous declines as a guide for what to expect in the future. Going into 2008, ASML stock was fairly valued and fell about -65% off its highs during a recession. In 2022, ASML was richly valued going into a non-recessionary environment and fell about -55% off its highs. After 2000, ASML was richly valued going into a recessionary environment, and the stock price fell -90%. While it's obvious that things are always changing over a 30-year period, this historical data is very useful for creating a broad framework in which we can think about ASML stock.

Peak P/E ratios & Super Cycles

While I pointed out earlier in the article that fundamental metrics like earnings were not good metrics for valuing deep cyclical businesses, examining peak P/E ratios that occur before earnings declines can give an indication of what the peak valuation was like going into a downturn. This is useful because I will be measuring drawdowns off peak prices, so it stands to reason that if a stock is overvalued going into a downturn, then it might fall deeper than a previous downturn in which it was not overvalued at the start of the downturn.

What is important for this investing strategy is to identify ways in which this downcycle might be different than historical downcycles. In particular, identifying potential super cyclical peaks is important. We can see that the most recent peak had a peak P/E ratio more than double the 2007 peak P/E. This could lead to a deeper downcycle if we have a similar recession as we had in 2008/9.

One additional way I identify supercycles is to simply look at a very long-term log scale price chart and watch for times of extreme upward movement.

When we do this, we see that the 2000 super cycle certainly stands out as a huge upcycle/bubble. The 2007 peak is identifiable as a peak mostly due to the 2008/9 drawdown rather than a huge runup in price ahead of time. In fact, the 2007 peak is more in line with the 2018 peak we saw for ASML. The two recent peaks in 2022 and 2024 look bigger than the 2007 and 2018 peaks, but not as big as the 2000 bubble. So, we probably have experienced a recent boom or super-cycle, but it didn't get as extreme as the one in the late 1990s.

In addition to the recent super cycle, we also need to consider whether the semiconductor industry has an underlying secular growth demand from the increasing use of technology in the modern world, including, of course AI, and the Internet of Things. I think there is plenty of evidence that even though ASML is a cyclical business, it is also in a secular growth industry. This can create a sort of counter-balance to the current downcycle even if it's coming off a super cyclical peak. My take is that the secular growth trend might cancel out some of the super-cycle risk, so the most likely outcome of ASML's current downcycle is somewhere in the middle, or, a "normal" downcycle that is significant, but likely not the worst we have seen historically.

Determining What Prices To Buy

Consistently investing and profiting over the medium term in deep cyclical businesses is difficult. It requires a unique investing approach. You will notice there are many details about ASML's business I have omitted in this article. This is not by accident. There are two important reasons I've done so. The first is that those details are not important. What is important is this is a business with a history of deep earnings cyclicality. The second reason is that often the more we get into the details of a business, the more false sense of security and confidence we have. We can convince ourselves that we know things about the future we do not or cannot know. As evidence of this, there have been 40 "Buy" or "Strong Buy" articles written on ASML this year in Seeking Alpha. Most of them will contain some sort of traditional analysis or deep company or industry insight. Those bullish positions are all underwater now on an absolute basis, and even worse on a relative basis compared to the S&P 500. Conversely, there have been 2 "Sell" articles published during the same time period. This isn't to say industry knowledge isn't important. It is. But it is dwarfed by the importance of understanding cyclicality, and not getting caught up in "stories" and media hype.

One of the things I love about deeply cyclical stocks is that they create situations where precision is unnecessary in order to get great returns. An investor doesn't have to know exactly how far a stock price will fall or exactly how long it will take for it to recover its old highs because even if an investor buys after a -50% drawdown, and it takes the stock 5 full years to recover to a price the market was willing to pay in the past, the returns will still be excellent. I've written dozens of articles about this over the years. The last two successful ones in the semiconductor industry were Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Micron (MU) in the fall of 2022, and they both went on to double in less than 2 years. Studying history and ignoring hype are important skills to have when investing in the stocks of cyclical businesses.

Because even during a time when there was no recession, in 2022, ASML has demonstrated it can fall more than -50% off its high, there is never a time I would consider buying this stock when the stock price wasn't at least -50% off its high. However, because there are secular growth trends that remain for their business, I would be willing to buy at that point if the decline gets that deep. So, my first buy price for ASML is at $555.05 per share, about -50% off its recent peak.

But because I know from studying history that ASML has fallen much deeper than that during a recession, I would also 1) be prepared mentally for the possibility that if I buy at $555 the stock could very easily fall much deeper in a recession, and 2) be willing to add to my position if the stock gets down to -65% off its highs.

Conclusion

It is standard practice for stock analysts to put out "price targets", where the goal is to try to predict where a stock price will be in 12 to 18 months. This is not how I approach investing. (Mostly because I don't think fundamentals are good at predicting short-term stock price movements, even if they are good at predicting longer-term stock prices.) My approach is to know the level of returns I would like to achieve in my portfolio over the long term, and to use that to determine the level of returns I think I can get from my stock investments. Once I know that, I can determine the prices for stocks at which my returns goals have a high probability of being met over the medium term of 3-5 years. These are my "Buy Prices". Because I monitor about 300-400 stocks that are high enough quality for me to buy if they hit the right price, I don't need for every stock to hit my buy price. I just need enough of them to do so. This means I don't need to chase stocks, and I can be very selective when it comes to quality and price. If ASML hits my buy price, I'll buy the stock, just like I did with MU and AMD in 2022. And if it doesn't. I will wait for some other opportunity.

data.title: TSLL: Get Out Ahead Of Tesla's Q3 Report, Bearish Post-Earnings Trends
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) shareholders have endured high volatility in the past handful of quarters. After bottoming at $102 in early 2023, Elon Musk’s darling stock surged to just shy of $300 in the summer of last year. TSLA peaked there, however, and a series of lower highs has ensued in what appears to be a broad consolidation period as the S&P 500 has trended decidedly higher.

Ahead of the Magnificent Seven member’s Q3 report due out on Wednesday, I have a sell rating on the Direxion Daily TSLA Bull 2X Shares ETF (NASDAQ:TSLL). This is technically a downgrade from a hold rating that I made when I last reviewed the fund in November of 2023. Of course, with a leveraged ETF, the typical holding period is very short.

Since Q4 last year, EV prices have soured. The mid-October read of the Manheim Used Vehicle Value Index revealed that EV prices on the secondary market are down by more than 12%, a drop that’s larger than all other used vehicle categories. It’s a headwind for the $705 billion market cap company, and I see downside risks post its Q3 profit report.

EV Prices Down Hard YoY

Investors should recognize the risks associated with owning a leveraged ETF like TSLL. I always like to remind investors, that long-term gains can be high if a protracted trend takes place, but volatility generally hurts such ETFs. Thus, a fund like TSLL should only be used as a short-term trading vehicle, not as a long-term investment, and hence, this article is a near-term tactical outlook on TSLA. For a deeper understanding of the risks tied to leveraged ETFs, you can refer to authoritative bodies such as the SEC. More information can be found in the updated Investor Bulletin titled: Updated Investor Bulletin: Leveraged and Inverse ETFs.

According to the issuer, TSLL seeks daily investment results, before fees and expenses, that are 150% of the performance of the common shares of Tesla. Direxion highlights a major risk with TSLL: Unlike traditional ETFs, or even other leveraged and/or inverse ETFs, these leveraged and/or inverse single-stock ETFs track the price of a single stock rather than an index, eliminating the benefits of diversification. Leveraged and inverse ETFs pursue daily leveraged investment objectives, which means they are riskier than alternatives that do not use leverage.

TSLL is a moderate-sized ETF with $2.0 billion in assets under management as of October 18, 2024. It carries a high 0.96% annual expense ratio and pays a high 6.7% forward dividend yield. Share-price momentum has been lukewarm in the past few months, which is not surprising given TSLA’s up-and-down price action. TSLL is a risky product, evidenced by a poor F rating, according to Seeking Alpha’s quantitative ETF grading system. Finally, liquidity metrics are sound given the fund’s high average daily trading volume of close to 50 million shares.

For a quick take on Tesla’s valuation and earnings outlook, the company trades with a high 96 forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings multiple while its price-to-sales ratio is actually much lower than its 5-year average – now at 7.1 on a forward basis versus a long-term average of 9.5.

Analysts expect $0.60 of normalized EPS to be reported on Wednesday afternoon, which would be about a 10% drop from $0.66 of operating EPS earned in the same period a year ago. Tesla has missed on earnings in each of the past four quarters, so I would not be surprised to see a soft bottom-line number.

Tesla Valuation Metrics: High, But Below Long-Term Averages

Q4 Deliveries Seen Near 546k

Whenever we are discussing short-term, earnings-related investment ideas, it’s helpful to analyze the options market and other historical trends. According to data from Option Research & Technology Services (ORATS), there’s a 6.2% earnings-related stock price swing implied from the current straddle pricing. That’s about a percentage point below the typical implied move of 7.3%.

What should frighten TSLA bulls is that the stock has traded lower post-earnings in five of the past six events, with each of those declines being more than 9%. That’s a bearish trend heading into the Q3 report.

Tesla's Implied Earnings Move is More than 6%, Weak Reaction History

It's not all bearish for the world’s most valuable EV automaker, however. October through February has historically been a strong stretch for TSLA shares, though the past two Octobers have been weak.

Thus, I assert that going short TSLL is a near-term strategy, while going long TSLA once it hits support could be a viable strategy.

Tesla Seasonality: Bullish Q4 Trends

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q3 2024 earnings date of Wednesday, October 23 AMC with a conference call immediately after the numbers hit the tape. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Tesla's Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With TSLA still priced richly compared to its industry, but somewhat less lofty on valuation versus its own history, the technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that TSLA has a clear downtrend resistance line that currently comes into play around $260. With a flat to slightly negatively sloped long-term 200-day moving average, the bears have an advantage today. Support potentially comes into play at both the 200dma just above $200 and in the mid-$100s.

Based on TSLA’s options pricing, a drop post-earnings to $200 may present a buying opportunity. So, the play would be to short or sell TSLL, then exit that trade if we see a 10% drop in TSLA shares.

Bigger picture, with neutral RSI momentum trends and a high amount of volume by price, I would not be surprised to see volatile price action with no clear trend over the coming weeks, making selling a leveraged long TSLA ETF a solid idea.

TSLA: Ongoing Consolidation, $260 Resistance, Support at the 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a sell rating on TSLL. Going short this leveraged long TSLA ETF would be a winning trade if the trend of poor earnings reactions persists and if TSLA stock continues to trade without a clear uptrend.

1) The Lowdown on Leveraged and Inverse Exchange-Traded Products (FINRA)

2) Leveraged and Inverse ETFs: Specialized Products with Extra Risks for Buy-and-Hold Investors (SEC)

3) FINRA's Reminder on sales practices for Leveraged and Inverse ETFs (FINRA)

data.title: QQQ: An Aggressive Bet On AI Adoption
data.description:
Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ) is a tech-focused ETF with considerable outperformance potential in the coming years as artificial intelligence products, both in the hardware and software markets will become available to a broader audience. The ETF further allows investors to build concentrated exposure to Mag 7 stocks, many of which, including NVIDIA (NVDA), have outperformed investor expectations in the last several years. The ETF is obviously only suitable for investors that have a favorable outlook on the technology industry and believe that adoption of artificial intelligence products is going to rise. Despite the near lack of diversification, I believe the ETF could help investors build a broad position in leading AI companies and benefit from growth in this specific market going forward.

Portfolio composition, long-term returns, and AI potential

The Invesco QQQ ETF tracks the Nasdaq 100 Index, which therefore means the ETF is chiefly investing in technology companies. The ETF manages approximately $295B in net assets, and the fund has massively benefited from the tech rally in the last several years. With Invesco QQQ ETF being focused on technology investments, and Mag 7 stocks especially, the ETF has managed to book some impressive returns in the last ten years.

In the last decade, the Invesco QQQ ETF delivered an average annual rate of return of 18.27%. In the last year, the ETF's NAV and price have surged more than 37% as the tech rally went into a new up-leg, driven by the potential for new AI-driven productivity gains in both the hardware and software market.

The Invesco QQQ ETF was established on March 10, 1999 and has, relative to its recent performance numbers, weaker life-to-returns. Since its inception, the Invesco QQQ ETF returned only about half the return it achieved in the last decade, 9.93% annually on average, which is due to the inclusion of the dot-com bust period in the early 2000s that led to a lost decade in tech.

The Invesco QQQ ETF has aggressive allocations to the largest tech names in the industry which is what one would expect from a Nasdaq 100-oriented ETF: Apple (AAPL) has the largest weighting in QQQ with a portfolio representation of 8.9%, followed by chip design company Nvidia with an 8.5% portfolio share and Microsoft (MSFT) which represents 7.8% of the fund’s investments.

Other Mag 7 stocks are also included (see the split below). Readers should note that Alphabet (GOOG), which I consider one of the most egregiously mispriced tech companies -- The Valuation Makes No Sense -- represented 4.7% of total portfolio investments, but its total position was split over different classes of Google's shares.

The main driver of the QQQ, in my opinion, will be the degree to which artificial intelligence products are going to get adopted by corporations and consumers in the future. Stock market indices just reached new highs, which are almost exclusively attributable to the boom in AI spending that has captured the fantasy not only of tech CEOs, but the average investor as well. This new AI industrial revolution has already had a material impact on the valuations of the Mag 7 stocks in the last three years.

The best-performing stock was Nvidia with a return of more than 519% during this time as the company is the ultimate beneficiary of AI-related chip growth. Nvidia benefits enormously from red-hot demand for AI GPUs and is set to launch its Blackwell GPU, which promises a massive leap in inference capabilities. Nvidia stated that the Blackwell GPU was already sold out for the next twelve months, indicating that the spending boom in AI GPUs is durable. The lack of an abundant supply of AI accelerators, at a time when demand is soaring, is why Nvidia has massive revenue outperformance potential in the next 24 months. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) just reported blow-out Q3'24 earnings, attesting to the same massive demand for AI chips: The Ultimate AI Weather Vane.

AMD’s CEO Lisa Su, to state yet another example of the upswing in the sector, recently said that it sees the Data Center GPU market grow to $500B in revenue by the end of FY 2028 which implies a 10X factor increase compared to FY 2023. AMD is set to be a major beneficiary of the AI GPU spending ramp as well, and the company is now my second-largest position in my portfolio for this very reason.

According to Precedence Research, the market for artificial intelligence (both hardware and software) is set to expand rapidly in the next decade. The market for AI is set to expand to 3.7T by the end of FY 2034, leading to an average annual growth rate of ~20%. This growth is going to be captured chiefly, in my opinion, by Mag 7 companies… many of which have dominant positions in digital advertising, autonomous and electric vehicles, robots, AI chips and consumer technology.

QQQ's valuation

The Invesco QQQ ETF has seen rapid growth in its net asset value in the last ten years and widely outperformed the S&P 500 as investors jumped on the opportunity to invest in large-cap tech companies that benefit from AI-related growth. In the last ten years, the Invesco QQQ ETF has delivered more than twice the returns than an equal-sized investment in an S&P 500 ETF returned.

Risks with QQQ

The biggest upshot of the Invesco QQQ ETF is that the fund is heavily concentrated in Mag 7 companies. In other words, if Mag 7 stocks continue to do well, the QQQ is set to see very strong net asset value growth. On the other hand, since the QQQ is so heavily focused on the technology industry, any underperformance of the sector could lead to a drastic drop in the ETF’s net asset value, exposing investors to steep valuation draw-downs.

Final thoughts

The Invesco QQQ ETF has put up some impressive performance numbers lately, and I don’t believe we are anywhere near the end of the current AI-related spending boom... this is the take-away from Nvidia's recent comments as well as TSMC's third-quarter earnings scorecard. Since the AI theme benefits no other sector more than the technology industry, I believe the Invesco QQQ ETF is worthy of a portfolio over-weighting and most certainly an ETF investors would want to buy on dips, in my opinion. The Mag 7-heavy profile of the Invesco QQQ ETF paired with a low expense ratio of 0.2% and historical outperformance makes QQQ a top bet for growth investors, especially those that have high expectations with regard to AI adoption going forward.

data.title: ClearBridge International Growth ACWI Ex-US Strategy Q3 2024 Commentary
data.description:
By Elisa Mazen, Michael Testorf, CFA & Pawel Wroblewski, CFA

Japanese Reforms Start to Yield Results

Market Overview

Broadening participation sent international equities higher in the third quarter, with value and cyclical shares leading the way as financial conditions eased across most developed markets. The benchmark MSCI All Country World Ex-U.S. Index advanced 8.06%, the MSCI ACWI Ex-U.S. Small Cap Index jumped 9.03%, while the MSCI Emerging Markets Index added 8.72%.

From a regional standpoint, Asia Ex Japan, North America and emerging markets outperformed the index. The United Kingdom performed in line with the index while Europe Ex U.K. and Japan underperformed in a period that saw currency volatility as a rising yen forced carry trade investors who were short the currency to cover their losses. Chinese equities surged to end the quarter (up 23.49%) following the announcement of aggressive stimulus from Beijing, lifting the performance of broader emerging markets.

Sharp reversals were the order of the day in the third quarter, with some of the worst-performing markets such as Hong Kong staging a sharp turnaround after the announcement of large stimulus packages from Chinese officials. Nearly a year's worth of trading took place in the last day of the quarter. This led China-focused segments, like materials, to make significant moves higher. Japan's choice of a new Liberal Democratic Party head to be prime minister also surprised the market, raising fears that he might not follow the corporate policies of the previous administration - something that has since been put to rest - and led to money going back into China from Japan from shorter-term players in the market. Year to date, Japan had been one of the better-performing markets, while China had been unquestionably the worst, hence the strong reversion.

Political drama in France continued with a new prime minister floating the idea of additional corporate tax increases to help salvage the expanding budget deficit; this kept French stocks on their back foot. The 50 basis point Fed rate cut rounded things out at the end of the quarter. Now markets are nervously anticipating the upcoming U.S. election and what that could mean for international trade. The U.S. dollar was broadly weaker throughout the quarter against all major currencies.

International growth stocks rose 6.92% for the quarter but underperformed their value counterparts by 234 basis points as a market rotation away from previous winners in health care and technology ensued.

Exhibit 1: MSCI Growth vs. Value Performance

The ClearBridge International Growth ACWI Ex-US Strategy underperformed the benchmark in a third-quarter environment favoring value shares. Our diversified focus beyond traditional, higher-growth, higher-beta stocks helped mitigate the degree of underperformance and we believe such an approach is well-suited for broadening markets. Performance was highlighted by strength in both our cyclical holdings in the consumer discretionary sector as well as information technology, although our overweight to the sector neutralized this advantage. Early signs of wider participation are encouraging for us as active, bottom-up growth managers.

Portfolio holdings that had led in a previously narrow international market environment, including GLP-1 pharmaceutical developer Novo Nordisk (NVO) and semiconductor equipment makers ASML and Tokyo Electron (OTCPK:TOELY), gave back gains in the rotation. While Tokyo Electron had been trimmed pretty heavily earlier in the year, we capitalized on late-quarter weakness to bring this position to a more active weight. We maintain confidence in these franchises as leaders in the secular growth market of diabesity treatments, the turnaround of the memory markets in semiconductors and the broadening adoption of Gen AI.

Results were also held back by weakness among the Strategy's industrial holdings, mainly British pest control provider Rentokil (RTO), which has seen disappointing growth thus far from its U.S. expansion strategy. Japanese industrial filter and valve maker SMC (OTCPK:SMCAY) detracted as well, due to ongoing sluggishness in the Chinese economy. We exited both stocks in favor of better-positioned industrial opportunities.

Portfolio Positioning

We were active in the quarter selling positions where we feel growth is on the decline, upgrading into new ideas with better growth and valuation support. At this point we are convinced that the Japan move to upgrade profitability, capital allocation and shareholder returns is authentic and added five new ideas that reflect this confidence.

In the secular bucket, we purchased Terumo (OTCPK:TRUMF), a Japanese manufacturer of medical equipment for blood management, cardiac and vascular procedures as well as general hospital uses. Its core cardiovascular and vascular device business is growing at above-market rates due to better patient outcomes and improved hospital economics. New product launches in its blood and cell technologies business should further bolster Terumo's growth. We see the company expanding margins through steady growth in core markets, new business and more aggressive M&A that is possible under its new CEO.

Structural growth companies provide us the opportunity to generate alpha from turnarounds and restructurings as well as broader cyclical drivers. The other four Japan additions fall into this category - Asics (OTCPK:ASCCF), Tokio Marine (OTCPK:TKOMY), Fujikara, an electrical equipment maker, and Nippon Sanso Holdings (OTCPK:TYNPF), a provider of industrial gases. All stand to benefit from both company-specific actions as well as broader reforms in the Japanese market aimed at improving return on equity and shareholder engagement while promoting M&A activity.

Asics is a specialty running shoe designer whose newer management is committed to bringing the business back to industry-like profitability. In addition to its iconic running shoes, the company also develops footwear for the sports lifestyle and health/comfort markets. With better operational focus, inventory management and a move to specialty sales channels, we believe Asics's well-known quality will allow it to sell more products with better pricing and margins, leading to significant earnings growth.

Tokio Marine is a top global property and casualty insurer. M&A among its smaller rivals in the past few years has created greater parity but Tokio Marine remains the largest insurer by market cap due to its larger overseas portfolio, mostly based in the U.S. We see three sources of price appreciation for the company going forward: premium increases in its domestic P&C business, the selling off of cross holdings that could unlock up to 30% of the company's current value and bolt-on acquisitions funded by its high cash generation.

The Bank of Japan's latest Tankan survey confirmed a continued recovery in domestic demand, rising GDP growth and a low risk of deflation returning, conditions conducive to our increasing exposure to the region.

While Beijing's late-quarter stimulus measures are a good start, ongoing weakness in the world's second-largest economy has caused us to pare back our direct holdings in the market over the past year. We are now evaluating our indirect exposure as well, which resulted in several exits and trims during the quarter, the largest being the sale of Hong Kong-based life insurer AIA Group (OTCPK:AAGIY). Although AIA's business has started to improve, wages and employment, particularly among younger consumers, have slowed dramatically, creating concern of less disposable income insurance purchases. Government stimulus through lower rates will also lower investment income for insurers.

One of our bigger sales was Nestle (OTCPK:NSRGY), the Swiss packaged foods giant, where growth appears to have peaked under former CEO Mark Schneider. Nestle's new CFO has indicated that upward earnings guidance will be pushed out further than anticipated. We exited the long-held name in favor of French food and beverage maker Danone (OTCQX:DANOY), a higher-growth idea in consumer staples.

Outlook

With its September rate cut, the Federal Reserve joined central banks in the eurozone, the U.K., Switzerland and Canada in easing financial conditions. The Fed move has ramifications well beyond the U.S. and is supportive of broadening participation across both developed and emerging equity markets. Combined with China's aggressive stimulus measures announced late in the quarter, we expect global economic activity will remain positive through the end of the year and into 2025.

Despite the strongly positive reaction by Chinese equities to Beijing's monetary easing, we believe more needs to be done to restart growth. While the measures are targeted predominantly at the equity markets and local government budgets, we need to see the creation of long-term demand drivers and consumer spending, both of which have been difficult during the housing market declines. China is expected to make additional policy moves at the end of October and with the outcome of U.S. elections soon thereafter, we should know more about the path of its recovery soon.

We are encouraged by the improving conditions across our investable universe as they create a more conducive environment for our diversified approach to growth. Recent performance has highlighted the benefits of complementing core secular growth positions with opportunistic ownership of structural growers and selective exposure to emerging growth names.

Portfolio Highlights

During the third quarter, the ClearBridge International Growth ACWI Ex-US Strategy underperformed its MSCI ACWI Ex-U.S. benchmark. On an absolute basis, the Strategy delivered gains across all nine sectors in which it was invested (out of 11 total), with the consumer discretionary, financials and consumer staples sectors the primary contributors.

On a relative basis, overall stock selection contributed to performance but was offset by negative sector allocation effects. In particular, stock selection in the IT and consumer discretionary sectors and a lack of exposure to energy drove results. Conversely, an overweight to IT, an underweight to financials and stock selection in the industrials and health care sectors detracted from performance.

On a regional basis, stock selection in Japan and the United Kingdom supported performance while stock selection in emerging markets and Europe Ex U.K. and an overweight to Europe Ex U.K. proved detrimental.

On an individual stock basis, the largest contributors to absolute returns in the quarter included Inditex in the consumer discretionary sector, Haleon (HLN) in the consumer staples sector, argenx (ARGX) and HOYA (OTCPK:HOCPY) in the health care sector and London Stock Exchange (OTCPK:LDNXF) in the financials sector. The greatest detractors from absolute returns included positions in Novo Nordisk in health care, ASML, Tokyo Electron and SK hynix (OTCPK:HXSCF) in IT and Rentokil in industrials.

In addition to the transactions mentioned above, we added new positions in Prysmian (OTCPK:PRYMF) in the industrials sector, Coupang (CPNG) in the consumer discretionary sector and repurchased Spotify (SPOT) in the communication services sector. The Strategy also exited positions in Antofagasta (OTC:ANFGF) and Givaudan (OTCPK:GVDBF) in the materials sector, Monday.com (MNDY) and Hexagon (OTCPK:HXGBF) in the IT sector, Daifuku (OTCPK:DFKCY) in the industrials sector and McDonald's Japan (OTCPK:MDNDF) in the consumer discretionary sector.

Elisa Mazen, Managing Director, Head of Global Growth, Portfolio Manager

Michael Testorf, CFA, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Pawel Wroblewski, CFA, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Copyright © 2024 ClearBridge Investments. All opinions and data included in this commentary are as of the publication date and are subject to change. The opinions and views expressed herein are of the author and may differ from other portfolio managers or the firm as a whole, and are not intended to be a forecast of future events, a guarantee of future results or investment advice. This information should not be used as the sole basis to make any investment decision. The statistics have been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but the accuracy and completeness of this information cannot be guaranteed. Neither ClearBridge Investments, LLC nor its information providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information.

Neither ClearBridge Investments, LLC nor its information providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information. Without limiting any of the foregoing, in no event shall MSCI, any of its affiliates or any third party involved in or related to compiling, computing or creating the data have any liability for any direct, indirect, special, punitive, consequential or any other damages (including lost profits) even if notified of the possibility of such damages. data.title: IBM Earnings Preview: Big Technical Breakout; Company Hasn't Repo'ed Any Stock Since March '19
data.description:
IBM (NYSE:IBM) is scheduled to report their Q3 ’24 financial results after the closing bell on Wednesday, October 23rd, 2024.

September ’24 saw the stock see a strong breakout above the April ’13 highs of $213-215, as evidenced by this Worden chart:

This is actually an 8-day chart of IBM, which allows the left-hand margin to go all the way back to 2013, so readers can see the old all-time high.

That’s also a breakout of an 11-year base (if you want to call it that), although IBM’s stock has a long history of performing like this, i.e., long periods of a range-bound stock price followed by a big breakout, which dates back to the 1980s.

IBM fundamentals and valuation

  • When IBM reports their third quarter earnings Wednesday night after the closing bell, consensus expectations will be looking for $2.23 in EPS, on $2.6 billion in operating income, and $15 billion in revenue for “expected” y-o-y growth of 1%, 15% and 2% revenue growth, respectively.

Looking for a fundamental metric that supports the breakout in the stock and the ramp in the shares in the last 90 days, operating income beat consensus in Q2 ’24 and had an “upside surprise” of 48%, with $2.87 billion actual versus the consensus estimate of $1.94 billion (at that time).

  • In the aforementioned Q2 ’24, IBM grew revenue +2%, operating income +16%, and EPS 11%, all y-o-y. The big takeaway from both Morningstar and the conference call notes was that IBM’s AI is at a $2 billion run rate (since inception, not YTD), and Arvind Krishna noted on the conference call that its AI business mix is 1/4th software and 3/4th consulting signings.
  • For Q4 ’24 (typically IBM’s strongest quarter of the year), the sell-side consensus is expecting $3.76 in EPS, $4.3 billion in operating income, and $17.9 billion in revenue for expected y-o-y growth given current estimates of -2% EPS, +16% operating income, and 3% revenue growth.
  • For full-year 2025, the current consensus is $10.72 and $66.3 billion in revenue for full-year expected growth of 5% and 5%.

Let’s see how the guidance looks for Q4 ’24 and for 2025.

Perhaps something the Street is warming up to is IBM’s improved free cash flow. Since the middle of last decade, when the company was routinely spending $500 million to $1 billion per quarter in capex ($3-4 billion per year), capex today has shrunk to a $1 billion per year run rate (as of the trailing twelve-month numbers), which means IBM’s free cash flow has improved without a commensurate improvement in cash flow. (This is somewhat anomalous to what other big tech companies are seeing, i.e., huge y-o-y growth in capex spending on AI, so maybe Arvind and the team have found a nice little niche within AI that can grow smartly and yet not require the huge capex outlays.)

IBM’s trading at 23x and 22x EPS for expected EPS growth this year and next year of around 5%, but the cash flow valuation metrics are just half that at 12x and 14x TTM cash flow and free cash flow. A few years ago, I was worried about the company’s quality of earnings (comparing net income to trailing cash flow and free cash flow), but today, IBM’s cash flow and free cash covers net income by 156% and 143% respectively.

No question cash flow and, more importantly, free cash flow has improved.

IBM hasn’t spent $2 billion on a share repurchase since the March 2019 quarter, and the share repurchases dried up entirely shortly following March ’19, but IBM could be ready to begin share repurchases again with their cumulative growth in free cash flow the last 2-3 years.

Trading at 23x trailing EPS and about that metric for ’24 and ’25 estimates, as well as 3x trailing price-to-sales, the 5% free cash flow yield and 2.75% look “meh” until you look at the cash flow valuations.

Summary / conclusion

Technically and fundamentally, at least from a free cash flow story, IBM has become a more interesting story in 2024. The technical breakout should be noted for technically inclined investors.

Ultimately, for IBM to be a stock that rivals some of the tech growth giants of today in forward expected stock price returns, it will need sustainable revenue growth in the high single digits (at least) and an occasional quarter of 10% revenue growth.

It’s not a bad thing that old tech is starting to stir. AI is giving every tech company with a software or consulting arm an opportunity to reinvent themselves.

Let’s see if IBM resumes share repurchases, but ultimately, a healthier IBM means consistent revenue growth and a viable AI strategy.

None of this is a recommendation or advice, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of futures results. Investing can, and does, involve the loss of principal, even for short periods of time. All EPS and revenue estimate data are sourced from LSEG.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ASML Holding: Share Price Is Still Lofty Despite The Most Recent 16% Plunge
data.description:
Introduction

On 15 October 2024, the share price of ASML Holding (NASDAQ:ASML) (OTCPK:ASMLF) plunged by 16% after information related to the company's 3Q24 results was published earlier due to a technical error. During this period, the company has lost about €49.2 billion in market capitalization.

Given that most of the world's semiconductor foundries are dependent on ASML, this may represent a good opportunity for investors to accumulate shares. However, based on my analysis, I believe that investors should hold their horses and not be overly excited. In this report, I will demonstrate why we should wait for the prices to further correct before starting to accumulate shares of ASML.

Latest Developments

In 3Q24, total revenues surged from €6.24 billion to €7.47 billion, representing a growth of 11.90% year-on-year. During this period, the company has sold 17 more lithography systems as compared to the same period last year. However, total net bookings plunged by 52% to €2.63 billion from €5.57 billion the same period last year. Gross margin declined from 51.87% the same period last year to 50.80% but was offset declines in operating expenses; total operating income margin remain at 32.70%.

Looking forward, the company expects 4Q24 sales to range between €8.8 billion to €9.2 billion and gross margin to deteriorate further to between 49% and 50%. Despite lower gross margin, the company expects to spend lower on R&D and SG&A costs, offsetting deteriorations in gross margin.

ASML's Growth Rate To Continue Deteriorating

Although developments in artificial intelligence is likely to continue providing tailwinds for demand, it seems clear that the company's growth has peaked. ASML's 4Q24 sales guidance suggests that the company will likely post a revenue of €28.2 billion, representing a growth of only 2.33% this year. The slowdown in growth is primarily due to a slower recovery in the traditional non-AI related markets.

Over the past three years, we have seen an average growth of around 25% per annum. Moreover, the company expects to generate between €44 billion and €60 billion in sales by FY2030, representing an average growth of 13% per annum. It is clear that the company's growth has declined, and it is unlikely that we will continue to see an elevated growth rate of about 25%. After all, in the latest earnings call, Christophe Fouquet, Chief Executive of ASML, had said, "without AI, the market would be very sad".

'China Issue' To Continue Weighing On ASML

Apart from ASML's latest revenue woes, the company is also facing a lot of challenges due to export controls. The Dutch government has aligned with the United States and will prevent ASML from selling the company's more advanced machines to China; even older machines will be subjected to this regulation. The Dutch's Prime Minister, Dick Schoof, will also not allow ASML to service China's equipment when the company's license expires this year.

More importantly, ASML's remains highly exposed to China. In 2024, close to half of the company's revenue is attributable to China. Despite stringent export controls and a slew of new regulations, the company's biggest market is still in China. Roger Dassen, ASML's Chief Financial Officer have stated that sales in China will normalize to 20% of total revenue from about 49%.

It is important to highlight that this is based on the current regulatory environment. If regulations further tighten, there is a strong likelihood that sales will further deteriorate, falling short of the company's projections.

Markets Are Still Pricing In A Premium

Even though the company has plunged by close to 16% since Tuesday, the current share price of €664.50 implies that the markets are extremely optimistic about the company's growth rate.

If we assume: (1) the company's gross margin eventually reaches its target of 60% by FY2030, (2) current share price of €664.50, and (3) all other margins (e.g. SG&A and R&D) to maintain at its current level, a reverse valuation analysis will suggest that the market is pricing in a revenue growth of 16% per annum between FY2025 and FY2030. In other words, markets are pricing in a premium and expects the company to beat its revenue growth rate by 3% per annum for the next six years. To put it into context, markets are expecting ASML to generate €70 billion in revenues as compared to the company's guidance of €60 billion.

ASML Is A Cash Generator And Has A History Of Giving Back To Its Shareholders

Do not get me wrong, ASML has been an exceptional company that has a strong balance sheet and strong liquidity profile. Moreover, the company has stable margins and has proved to be able to effectively generate sustainable cashflows over the years. Over the past 10 years, the company has generated €35 billion in free cash flow.

Moreover, the company has a strong history of giving back to its shareholders. Over the past five years, the company's share buyback plan has reached a cumulative share buyback of €22.80 billion. ASML has also given back about a total of 10 billion in dividends over the past five years.

Valuation Based On Management's Guidance Suggests Share Price Is Lofty

Given that the management has provided so much color, I have utilized these inputs to conduct a valuation analysis of ASML's share price. My valuation model will be based on the following inputs: (1) revenue growth of 2.33% in FY2024 and 13.41% between FY2025 to FY2030, (2) gross margins to improve from 51.29% in FY2023 to 60% by FY30, (3) other margins such as SG&A and R&D to maintain at current levels, and (4) WACC of 8.57% and terminal growth rate of 5%.

Based on these assumptions, which are fairly generous with a terminal growth rate of 5%, the valuation model based on discounted cashflows suggests that the company's implied share price of €572.52. Based on the current share price of €664.50, it does seem that the price of ASML is still quite lofty.

Closing Remarks

ASML's share price has declined by about 16% in the most recent sell off, indicating a 33% of decrease from the company's peak in July 2024. Given the current artificial intelligence hype train and investors' interest in semiconductor related stocks, it is not surprising that many are using this opportunity to accumulate shares.

However, I believe that market participants are still pricing in a strong premium in ASML's shares. Using inputs from the company's management, the implied share price is 572.52, representing a potential further downside of 13.84%. Given the lack of any margin of safety, investors should not get overly excited; I believe that it will be wise for us to hold out longer before we see the value that we want to capitalize.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ASP Isotopes Up On Radiopharmaceutical And Nuclear Fuel Demand
data.description:
ASP Isotopes (NASDAQ:ASPI) was up sharply on Thursday after it was reported that the company had successfully enriched Ytterbium-176, a stable isotope used in oncology treatment, via its first "quantum laser enrichment" (CLE) facility, months ahead of schedule. Ytterbium-176 is an isotope that is in high demand due to Novartis' blockbuster, Pluvicto, and other radiopharmaceutical drugs. It is also a remarkable "proof of concept" for the company's faster production ramp for nuclear fuel.

The isotope market rarely gets the headlines, but that may change as companies like ASPI find themselves as essential suppliers to the US and Europe, as the West seeks to pull itself from decades of Russian dependence. Deploying its own proprietary processes, ASPI will be selling into high margin markets -radiopharmaceuticals and nuclear power - and facing limited competition. With its QLE process, it may have found a "better mousetrap," providing cheaper high-assay low-enriched uranium (HALEU) for the burgeoning small modular reactor market and reducing environmental hazard to boot.

Innovation and geopolitics make for a disruptive cocktail, but that fizz is the sound that opportunity makes. The two factors don't show up in quantitative models of stock performance - perhaps only as a slight "tremor in the force" - but they have enormous consequence when they rear up. Creative destruction and deconstructed supply chains can hurt.

ASP Isotopes is an interesting but overlooked micro-cap. It is poised to disrupt two significant markets, and it is doing so at just the right geopolitical moment. Its market set-up, customer list, and expected spin-out of Quantum Leap Energy make the company worth exploring.

The Tectonic Plates Of A Geopolitical Setup:

From medical science to quantum computing and SMR nuclear fuel production, isotopes will quietly play an increasing role in our society. The isotope supply chain is rarely discussed, but geopolitically it is one of the most fraught at present.

Isotopes are recognized as critical materials by the US Department of Energy (DoE), but our biggest supplier remains Isotope JSC, a subsidiary of Russia's Rosatom State Nuclear Energy Corporation (of Uranium One fame), that supplies more than 370 customers in 50 countries. Russia controls 85% of the world medical isotope market, with the Netherlands nearly a 15% stake.

The isotope and nuclear fuel markets are coming into relief this year due to specific US legislation. As discussed in a prior article, the West is now pushing Russia out of its nuclear supply chain entirely. In May, the Biden administration has signed into law the "Prohibiting Russian Uranium Imports Act." This new law bans imports of unirradiated low-enriched uranium from the country, starting on August 13, 2024, with various waivers applied until January 1, 2028. This response to the Ukrainian invasion and the US near-full dependence on Russian uranium and enrichment. The House Committee Report acknowledges that state-owned Rosatom and its subsidiary Tenex supply over 20% of the enriched uranium used in US reactors.

In addition to the Russia angle, there is another international wrinkle that lends ASPI favor. Long ago, ASP Isotopes acquired two isotope facilities from the South African company Klydon, a firm that was involved in the country's nuclear efforts in the 1960s, but which shifted its focus to commercial isotope separation following the closure of South Africa's nuclear program.

ASPI's existing South African facilities give it the opportunity to produce radioactive isotopes without the multi-year compliance issues for startups in the US or Europe proper. Nuclear enrichment is the most heavily regulated industry in the world, even more than medical isotope production. Years of underinvestment in the West as it relied on cheap Russian product has led to an impasse. This gives the market a definitive "barrier to entry," and ASPI a leg up.

Corporate Structure:

To better understand the opportunities at work, it is good to look the company's corporate structure. Delaware-based ASPI has several entities that handle different aspects of the operation.

  • ASP Isotopes (GUER): Most customer contracts operate through a Guernsey-based operation that also holds the South African operations as well as the factory in Iceland that is coming online next year.
  • ASP Isotopes (UK): intellectual property is domiciled in the UK.
  • PET Labs: ASPI owns a 51% stake in PET Labs, a South African operation which convert stable isotopes into radioisotopes for medical practitioners. This relationship gives ASPI a downstream conduit to sell lower cost isotopes into the growing radiopharmaceutical market.
  • Quantum Leap Energy: This wholly owned entity controls ASPI's nuclear isotope division. ASPI intends to spin it off to investors within the year on a national US exchange, and distribute a portion of the common equity to ASPI shareholders of record.

Implicit within this re-structuring is the understanding that the nuclear fuel business and the medical isotope business are different regulatory environments requiring different management skillsets.

It is also important to understand that there are two proprietary technological processes at work: the Aerodynamic Separation Process and the Quantum Laser Enrichment Process.

  • ASPI's Aerodynamic Separation Process (ASP) can isolate isotopes from gaseous elements. By blasting air within a stationary wall centrifuge, ASP can precisely capture of the target isotopes by directing them into a containment material. The hook is that the process excels in separating molecules with a mass of 100 atomic units or less, i.e. lighter elements. Traditional separation technologies were only effective with higher mass elements.
  • ASPI's Quantum Laser Enrichment (QLE) is a separate process. It deploys lasers to separate isotopes based on their transition energies with very high selectivity and in a cost-effective way. Quantum Leap Energy as a joint venture will use this process to focus on producing HALEU (the fuel used for the new small module reactor type) Lithium-6 (essential for nuclear fusion), while I believe ASPI will use it to create products like Ytterbium-176 (growing radiopharmaceutical use).

The October 17th news release was an enheartening proof of concept for the QLE technology. The thermodynamic similarities between ytterbium and uranium suggest that its HALEU deployment will also be successful, particularly as it appears to provide the lowest cost for HALEU production with a more flexible, modular plant design. That is why the stock shot up!

Also, important for an investor: the QLE process can re-enrich depleted uranium tails, essentially converting the 1.7 million metric tons of depleted uranium that is now a costly environmental hazard into a resource. By securing depleted uranium at low cost, QLE could provide far lower pricing.

This may give Quantum Leap Energy a real margin advantage against competitors like Centrus Energy (LEU) -another excellent company, but that uses a more traditional process that creates environmental hazard.

Supply And Demand: By Isotope

ASP third factory in South Africa and its fourth in Iceland will help address the West's supply needs -both medical/industrial as well as HALEU-related - and contracts are being secured.

ASPI's focus spans at least twelve isotopes, with medical and agricultural being first supplied. The following graphic -which I have compiled from the company's September presentation deck - breaks down their supply rollout:

Radiopharmaceuticals - a class of radioactive substances for diagnostic or therapeutic use - and HALEU are now high margin. The prolonged Ukrainian War has really diminished western supplies of Ytterbium-176 from Russia. As mentioned earlier, this is an isotope that is in high demand due to Novartis' blockbuster, Pluvicto, and other radiopharmaceutical drugs.

I find the following graphic below - provided by the company-as very helpful, as it charts out the various diagnostic and therapeutic radiopharmaceutical products that are on the market.

The company's news release on October 17 says it expects to have an annual production capacity of about 1 kilogram of highly enriched Ytterbium-176 per year, starting in in early 2025. The firm also has commitments to supply a minimum of $2.5 million worth of Carbon-14 annually for pharmaceutical use, as well as Silicon-28 and Germanium 70/72/74 for quantum computing deployments.

ASPI is presently the world's only commercial supplier of Silicon-28, the demand for which is expected to increase as semiconductor makers switch to Silicon-28 nanowires for performance gains (as its thermal conductivity limit is 60% higher).

Industrial isotope demand, however, is only the beginning for ASPI. In November 2023, ASPI signed a memorandum of understanding with a next-gen reactor company to establish a facility for producing high assay low-enriched uranium (HALEU).

Nuclear Power And The Spinoff Opportunity

The success of the Y-176 enrichment via its first quantum enrichment facility provides a clearer roadmap to how ASPI might fit in the HALEU supply chain. HALEU is the fuel for the next-gen SMR reactors, but there is virtually no US domestic capacity (from either government or commercial sources). Again, the Ukrainian War stripped supply out of the chain.

This, in turn, is crimping the build out of new reactors. One of the keystones of Biden's green initiative is swapping out coal plants for SMR reactors. The Nuclear Energy Institute (NEI) estimates that domestic demand for HALEU could surpass 600 metric tons by 2035, compared to nearly zero today. But in late 2022, Bill Gates' TerraPower actually postponed the launch of their small modular reactor due to a shortage of HALEU. With no active domestic producers, HALEU prices have surged to nearly $20,000 per kilogram.

Because the QLE process can re-enrich depleted uranium tails, essentially converting the 1.7 million metric tons of depleted uranium that is now a costly environmental hazard into a resource, QLE could provide far lower pricing when its facility comes online.

If ASPI's CLE process can produce HALEU profitably at a price below $10,000 per kilogram, this may prove to be far lower than western competitors. This may give Quantum Leap Energy a real margin advantage against Centrus Energy.

The decision to spin off Quantum Leap Energy to shareholders is a recognition that despite overlapping technical processes, HALEU production and industrial/ medical isotopes are separate business models in need of focused management, each with its own distinct knowledge base.

Financials And Risk

ASPI is presently a tiny three-year-old company, a micro-cap (293.169M) with a mere 66 million shares outstanding. Its manufacturing is just coming online this year and next. Traditional metrics like earnings, sales, or cash flow don't apply, yet. Profitability will likely not come until 2026-27 at the earliest.

There are many risks involved in a speculative company like ASPI that should not be taken lightly. New regulatory issues could develop. Project financing might fall through. There might a far slower transition to SMR reactors, and competitors like Silex Systems (OTCQX:SILXY) or Centrus Energy may lock up most of the HALEU demand. Lantheus (LNTH) might start producing isotopes for third parties, well beyond their own medical imaging business. Regime change in Russia might alter all restrictions; Chinese producers might also swamp the market.

The only positives for ASPI: it may have a "better mousetrap," there are clear secular tailwinds as Russia exits the Western markets, and its recent public offering in July (12 million shares at $2.50) would suggest that dilution is not a concern into the spin-off date.

Another difficulty in assessing a valuation is that the medical isotope business and the HALEU spin-off offer up distinct valuations. The global medical isotope market generated $5.1 billion in 2022 and is expected to jump to $11.4 billion by 2032. At present, ASPI will be selling into a looming medical isotope shortage. A reactor in the Netherlands (HFR in Petten) just went off-line for maintenance, affecting Mo-99 and Tc-99, another reflection of US dependence on foreign producers.

At present, the industrial business is worth an additional $2.00 a share while Quantum Leap, based on domestic HALEU market demand and its customer base (M of U, etc.), could easily be worth $11 a share. Perhaps more if the spin-out is bought.

Quantum Leap Energy is receiving interest. According to a November 27, 2023 news release, ASPI has received interest from potential customers totaling $30 billion worth of HALEU, over the period of 2027 to 2037. In April, ASP Isotopes Inc reported that it was in contract with a US-based SMR company for $2 million for an enrichment facility. This is the second of two, with SMR companies related to supply. (To streamline operations, ASPI management has moved the two contract/MOUs under the QLE joint venture as part of the plans to spin out the nuclear fuel business.) In connection with the spin-out, ASPI has a license agreement with QLE to produce enriched Uranium 235 and Lithium 6 in exchange for a perpetual royalty in the amount of 10% of all future QLE revenues.

Despite its obvious risks, ASPI has the tech and some favorable secular tailwinds. It also has an experienced CEO. Before co-founding ASPI in 2021, Paul Mann, spent 20 years on Wall Street investing in the healthcare and chemicals space at Soros Fund Management, Highbridge Capital, and Morgan Stanley. It's not exactly his first rodeo.

At this point, the company is in that classic "post-prototype/ pre-revenue" period, that zone where there is both the greatest opportunity and the greatest risk for an investor. Tread carefully and keep an eye on it. It should retreat in price following last week's run, offering a better price before the official spin-out announcement.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 10/21/24
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.
