data.title: Symbotic Has Plenty Of Room To Grow
data.description:
Introduction

Symbotic (NASDAQ:SYM) provides robotics solutions for e-commerce companies, mainly around warehousing management and automation to increase efficiency, speed, and accuracy of fulfillment. They provide software for robots that can work together to organize warehouses, sort packages, and optimize output within mixed-SKU pallets to quickly load trailers for customer delivery. Along with saving time for clients, their robots are also more capable with less footprint, leading to between 30-60% reduction in building space needed.

Their system operates dozens of autonomous robots that move around in a warehouse, moving and unloading shipments and leveraging AI software to optimize the location, layout, and structure of storage units. The system can forecast which boxes need to be shipped to which customers, and create workflows that minimize traffic, turnaround time, and accidents within a warehouse. In short, the SYM platform helps to dramatically improve operational efficiencies and accuracy in a warehouse setting.

Current customers include United Foods, Target, Walmart, Albertsons, C&S Wholesale Grocers, and a variety of other large distribution companies across 6 industries - Retail Grocery, Retail General Merchandise, Food Wholesale, Footwear & Apparel, Consumer Packaged Goods, and Food & Beverage.

SYM is aiming to provide the future of robotics software, which should lead to possible licensing plays down the road as they advance their logistics software capabilities even further. Industry analysts say that SYM and Softbank's GreenBox joint venture system is a "day and night" difference with the technologies they are replacing in warehouses. We are bullish on the future of SYM, acknowledging that it is a riskier investment for bold investors, and believe in the long-term vision of this company. This article will elaborate on our beliefs.

Macro Factors

There are 3 macro factors and external trends that, we believe, will help push SYM into further growth in the future. They are 1) continued rise of e-commerce, 2) additional use cases beyond warehouses, and 3) improved artificial intelligence capabilities in refining their system.

E-Commerce Growth:

Based on a variety of sources, e-commerce growth has been strong and is projected to continue increasing into the future. From Bloomberg: e-commerce will grow at a 10% CAGR to over $2.5T over the next 5 years. It currently represents roughly 25% of all US retail sales, and is forecasted to grow to over 33% by 2027. This growth results from typical online merchants (e.g., Amazon) but also the further proliferation of traditional brick and mortar stores conducting more and more business online. The graph below highlights the climb of e-commerce - a market that should continue rising over time.

Additional Robotics Use Cases:

Beyond the typical warehouse, SYM's products can be used in ports (all top 10 US ports have adopted some form of automation technology to track and move containers, and 4 have implemented automated cargo handling equipment), within large stores themselves (Sam's Club, Costco, BJ's), and manufacturing facilities. If they are able to execute on the CEO's vision of becoming an operating system for all robots of its kind, the applications for SYM are endless. CEO Rick Cohen has stated that the goal is for SYM to develop "Cloud Computing for the physical world" - in that there will be reduced inventory and costs while boosting SKU count and accuracy. The company is trying to create a smart network among its warehouses and supply chains, and that will help to catalog and train its AI models further and further with its capabilities and skills.

To further its reach, SYM has also been acquisitive - recently announcing their purchase of Veo Robotics, a company that intelligently safeguards industrial robots' maneuverability and helps to assist in the human and robot collaboration. It's clear that SYM is actively looking to continue developing its foundation and reach of skills for its robots, and as long as management is able to stay the course, the vision is impressive.

Further AI Capabilities:

Currently, SYM's AI capabilities focus on control / movement, task management automation, and smart palletization algorithms. Each of these help the robots to move safely around a warehouse, handle an extensive array of packages and containers, orchestrate shipping logistics, and optimize pallets for density, stability, shipment delivery timelines, and more. We believe that this is still just the beginning for SYM's growth, especially with its AI models. The more the AI learns, the more powerful it gets. Recently, SYM just announced a partnership with Walmart in Mexico to distribute their robots at 2 centers, one of which will mark the largest system SYM has deployed in a single phase project. Continued testing and results in the field will help to further develop its AI capabilities and improve its foundation for future clients.

Backlog And Financial Forecast

Based on SYM's latest earnings call, they have a backlog of $22.8B in orders that need to be delivered. Using this backlog, we have created a conservative forecast for the company through the next 6 years.

Let's break down the above forecast line by line:

Backlog Attainment/Revenue

According to SYM's most recent 10-K filing, their backlog at the time was to be delivered over 6 years. Given that, we are going to assume the current backlog also will be for deliveries over 6 years. We are also assuming that SYM cannot deliver on 1/6th of the backlog per year immediately, but will instead increase their deliveries over time as they grow their operations. As such, we have started with a 7% backlog attainment in year 1 which grows over time to 25% in year 6.

In order to keep the forecast conservative, we are assuming that the only revenue SYM will receive is from the current backlog. In reality, we would expect additional revenue to be booked/delivered between now and 6 years from now.

COGS, Other OpEx, and Depreciation

SYM is a growing company, so their current cost structure is not indicative of the long term. Given their larger size and comparable products, we are using Rockwell Automation's (ROK) cost structure to model SYM's long-term prospects. In order to come up with a reasonable comparison, we used ROK's numbers as of their 9/30/2020 yearly filling because it represented a similar revenue number to our forecast for SYM in 6 years. For this forecast, we assumed that SYM's COGSs, Other OpEx, and Depreciation as a percent of revenue would move towards ROKs proportions on a straight line basis over 6 years.

EV and EBITDA

Using all of the above assumptions, we arrive at an EBITDA forecast of $1.49 billion in 6 years. After applying the industry average EV/EBITDA ratio of 9.0, we forecast that SYM's EV in 6 years will be $13.4 billion. This represents a total growth of 509% from the current EV, which is equivalent to a 36% CAGR.

Valuation

We came up with an estimated current valuation for SYM by using their projected EV of $13.4 billion in 6 years.

After discounting the projected 6-year EV using SYM's weighted average cost of capital [WACC], we arrive at a current enterprise value of $4.9B, which works out to $55.75 per share. This is an ~85% increase from the current share price.

Recent Downtrend Since Last Earnings Is A Speculative Buying Opportunity

When SYM last reported earnings in July, they reported fiscal third quarter revenue of $492M, which beat analyst estimates of $465M. However, they missed EPS targets by 1 cent, and Q4 guidance fell short of estimates at between $455-$475M for this coming quarter. The CEO mentioned that system gross margins dipped below expectations due to longer construction schedules and higher implementation costs, but a key priority moving forward is improving performance by improving speed and expense of implementations.

Despite this, there were positive changes from the same quarter in the previous year. Operating loss improved by 40%, net loss narrowed by 64%, and earnings per share, while still negative, improved by 71%. These factors were driven by their 56% year-over-year increase in revenue in their systems group, and this trend of narrowing losses helps us believe that they will reach GAAP profitability in the coming few years. We view this as a beneficial opportunity to jump into the stock at these depressed levels, and believe that for the speculative longer-term investor, this stock can be worth multiples more in the future. Since it operates debt free, SYM is able to fully put its cash flows back into the business, instead of paying down expensive lines of credit.

Conclusion

SYM is a speculative buy at these depressed prices. Investors might face some up and downs as companies like this are more volatile, but we believe that due to 1) the variety of macro factors acting as a tailwind, 2) the strength of their backlog and continued operational improvements, and 3) lower trading levels at current prices, the stock would be a great buy and hold candidate for investors with a high-risk tolerance. The company has marquee clients and investors backing it, growth is very strong, and management seems to have a great vision for the future of the firm. With any company like this, it will be very important to continually monitor their backlog and latest news to ensure any changes to the thesis are identified, but at the current time, SYM can be a risky but potentially lucrative investment.

data.title: GSST: Goldman Sachs' Short-Term Bond Fund
data.description:
Thesis

Coming from Goldman Sachs Asset Management, the Goldman Sachs Access Ultra Short Bond ETF (BATS:GSST) represents the fixed income offering in the short duration bond space from GS. The fund seeks to provide current income with capital preservation, via a portfolio of investment grade bonds and MBS securities. The ETF is fairly new, having IPO-ed only in 2019.

While there are many choices in the short bond space, large asset management platforms tend to have the most compelling offerings, relying on their unparalleled platform research capabilities and human capital. To that end, we will show you how GSST has outperformed versus a cohort of peers.

In today's article we are going to have a closer look at GSST, its portfolio and structuring, and benchmark this short-term bond fund versus its peers.

Fund composition

Given its mandate and objectives, the name focuses on short dated investment grade bonds, MBS securities and Treasuries:

IG bonds represent 37% of the fund, followed by MBS securities at 24% and ABS bonds at 24%. From a ratings perspective, most names are AAA or AA:

The fund splits the government backed treasury and Agency securities in a separate sector, but we can consider them AAA for the purpose of a ratings matrix. The riskiest IG bonds, namely BBB rated ones, represent only 7.5% of the holdings in this fund. Please note, the above parsing denotes a fund which runs very little credit risk, with the name being mostly affected by rates in the short term. Its low duration also allows for a low probability of default for the residual corporate risk.

The name comes with a 0.56 years duration and a 4.94% 30-day SEC yield. Additional analytics are as follows:

  • AUM: $0.6 billion
  • Sharpe Ratio: -0.34 (3Y)
  • Std. Deviation: 1.1 (3Y)
  • Yield: 4.94% (30-day SEC)
  • Leverage Ratio: 0%
  • Composition: Treasuries, MBS bonds, IG bonds
  • Duration: 0.56 years
  • Expense Ratio: 0.2%

How does it stack up against the competition?

The fund falls in a cohort that includes many other competitors from the largest asset management platforms around. In fact, the Seeking Alpha platform has a dedicated tab for all ultra-short bond funds that an investor can consult here.

The main aspects to consider when looking at GSST and its competitors are the ones regarding yields and duration considerations. In a monetary loosening environment (today's environment), an investor wants to have as much duration as possible for a well-built short-term fund.

When looking on a 1-year period, GSST has done very well against its peers:

The fund has beaten on a 1-year look-back the PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity Active ETF (MINT), the JPMorgan Ultra-Short Income ETF (JPST) and the SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL). In fact, GSST's active management has allowed for a rough 50 bps outperformance versus its closest peer, a figure which is very significant when considering the low yielding nature of the asset class.

BIL is the lowest performer since it only contains risk-free treasuries, all while the rest of the names are overweight corporate bonds and MBS securities. While small, the incremental credit risk taken by GSST does provide for an additional yield enhancement.

What is the forward for GSST?

The fund is a short duration bond one, thus fully dependent on short-term yields. With the Fed having started its easing campaign, the front end of the curve has moved lower, and is expected to continue to decrease. All short dated bond fund will be affected, but with a certain lag. Ultimately, GSST provides for a spread over short-term treasury yields; thus the yield equation is short-term treasury yields plus credit spread. As short-term yields move lower, GSST's 30-day SEC yield will decrease as well.

We believe GSST will continue to deliver outsized value for its cohort via active management and security selection, but do not expect all-in yields to remain constant or increase here. It is a well-built fund, but a fund that cannot escape the overall macro theme of lower front-end rates. We like the fund's granular build with a solid ratings band composition, with GSST representing an attractive choice in the space.

While the next 12 months will see very aggressive Fed cuts as per the SOFR forward curve, the market does see a bottoming out in rates at a 3.2% level:

Courtesy of Chatham, we can see above the 1-month forward SOFR curve, a curve which bottoms out at 3.2% currently after January 2026. This simply means that the forward market sees long-term neutral rate levels at above 3%. The forward market is actively traded, and these levels will change.

The current picture, however, allows us to imply a long term 30-day SEC yield for GSST of roughly 4% as things stand, i.e. another 100 bps drop from the current level. Kindly keep in mind that GSST has a credit component, thus will always yield more than Fed Funds or SOFR for that matter, as we have seen from its performance graph.

Conclusion

GSST is a fixed income exchange-traded fund. The name is the Goldman offering in the short-term bond space, and comes with the research capabilities provided by the GS platform. The ETF has outperformed a cohort of peers in the past year, and has favorable analytics. Despite its pedigree, the fund is an expression of short-term rates with a credit spread on top, and will offer a decreasing yield as Fed Funds move lower. We like GSST and its build, and believe the ETF represents a robust choice in the short-term fund space. Investors, however, should be fully comfortable with a name that will continue to see its yield decrease to a level which we predict to be 4% based on the market priced new neutral rate.
